title: The Reverse Interview
prompt: I'm about to start this project. Interview me until you have 95% confidence about what I actually want, not what I think I should want.
why_it_works: AI asking YOU questions is 10x more powerful than you asking AI questions. This forces Claude to deeply understand your intent before building anything.
usage_notes: Use this at the very start of any project or feature. Let the AI ask multiple rounds of questions. Don't rush to the build phase — the interrogation IS the work.

title: The Rubber Duck Debugger
prompt: I'm going to explain this bug to you. Don't suggest fixes yet — just ask me clarifying questions until we've identified the root cause together. Then and only then, suggest solutions.
why_it_works: Jumping straight to solutions skips diagnosis. This forces a shared understanding of the problem before any code changes, dramatically reducing fix-then-break cycles.
usage_notes: Paste your bug description, error message, and relevant code. Resist the urge to accept the first fix offered — the questions phase is where the real insight happens.

title: The Steel Man
prompt: I'm about to make this decision: [decision]. Steel man the strongest possible case against it. Don't hold back — I want the best argument for why I'm wrong.
why_it_works: Most AI use reinforces what you already think. This deliberately inverts that. The strongest counterargument you haven't heard is the most valuable one.
usage_notes: After getting the steel man, ask: 'Given this, what would you do?' The combination of challenge + recommendation is unusually powerful for high-stakes decisions.

title: The Blank Page Killer
prompt: I need to write [piece]. Don't write it for me. Instead, ask me 5 questions that will unlock what I actually want to say. Then give me the single most important sentence to start with.
why_it_works: Writer's block is almost always a clarity problem, not a writing problem. Answering questions forces the thinking that makes writing easy. One strong first sentence breaks the paralysis.
usage_notes: Works for emails, essays, docs, posts, or any long-form content. After the first sentence, you can either continue alone or ask Claude to draft the full piece from your answers.

title: The Socratic Code Review
prompt: Review this code, but only ask questions — don't make statements. Each question should make me think about something I might have missed or could improve.
why_it_works: Statement-based reviews create defensiveness. Questions create curiosity. A question like 'What happens if this is null?' teaches more than 'This will throw a NullPointerException.'
usage_notes: Paste your code after the prompt. This format is especially valuable for learning contexts or when reviewing your own code — it surfaces blind spots without creating a list of 'errors.'

title: The Research Synthesizer
prompt: I've been reading about [topic]. Don't explain it back to me. Instead: what are the 3 things that most people get wrong about this, and what's the one mental model that unlocks deep understanding?
why_it_works: Standard research summaries give you what you already know. Misconceptions and mental models give you the non-obvious insights that change how you think about a topic.
usage_notes: Works best after you've already done some reading. The 'most people get wrong' framing tends to surface genuinely surprising insights rather than standard explanations.
